one_line_takeaway: Passing differential-privacy or membership-inference audits does not bound an LLM API's verbatim data-extraction risk, so the paper defines a separate cost-based (l,b)-inextractability metric (2^b required queries) and shows empirically that MIA-safe models can still leak Enron/Pile/BookSum text, while combining instruction-tuning, ParaPO post-training and API access control (limiting top-m token exposure) cuts extractable content from 20% to 0.03% at b=32.
key_finding: Indistinguishability and inextractability are formally incomparable: a model can pass strict differential-privacy or membership-inference audits yet still have high absolute extraction probability p1 (illustrated on Enron-fine-tuned GPT-2, Figure 2/3), and conversely low-extraction-risk samples can still be confidently distinguished by MIA. Correlation between MIA signals and extraction cost is strong for uncalibrated signals (Loss, Min-K) but weak for calibrated ones (Zlib, Ref-Loss), because calibration removes the absolute-confidence component that extraction depends on. The proposed estimator dominates existing measurements: empirical greedy decoding is deterministic and underestimates risk due to short/chunked prefixes, and the (n,p)-discoverable baseline saturates -- its extraction ratio caps at about 0.015 even at n=10^5 because tokens ranked beyond the truncation size k can never be sampled -- while the paper's rank-based bound scans all sliding windows and is tight for greedy decoding and a valid upper bound for any decoding configuration. Text-level factors driving extractability differ from those driving MIA vulnerability: low-complexity/low-diversity text (low Zlib, low TF-IDF, low Shannon entropy) and high-uniqueness text (low MinHash similarity, low kNN density) drive extraction risk, whereas MIA risk is dominated by per-sample reference-vs-target perplexity improvement, largely independent of complexity -- meaning MIA-based audits can miss the samples at highest worst-case extraction risk. On defenses: combining Tulu-3 instruction tuning with ParaPO reduces the extractable portion at b=32 from 20% (base Llama-3.1-8B) to 0.4%; adding API access control (limiting m) on top of Tulu+ParaPO further reduces it to 0.03%. DP training on GPT-2/Enron reduces extraction ratio but does not scale consistently across orders of magnitude of epsilon, especially for short sequences (l=10), empirically confirming the paper's Theorem 2 that DP resilience does not guarantee extraction resilience. Over 99% (99.29%) of protected subsequences fall below the in-context ('repeat after me') baseline cost b_ctx, indicating genuine training-data memorization rather than trivial context echoing. Across 483,485 Pile-CC subsequences, the Llama-3.1-8B target model's extraction cost exceeds (i.e. is riskier than) that of a GPT-2 proxy model in 398,385 cases (82.4%), consistent with larger models having lower extraction costs (Figure 13 right).
core_contribution: The paper (1) formally proves that indistinguishability-based privacy games (differential privacy distinguishability, membership inference, reconstruction, attribute inference) are neither sufficient nor necessary for bounding data-extraction risk in black-box LLM APIs -- i.e. the classical 'privacy reducibility chain' (DPD <= RC <= MI ~ AI) does not extend to data extraction (Theorem 2: DPD not reducible to DE; Theorem 3: DE not reducible to DPD); (2) introduces a new cryptography-style security definition, (l,b)-inextractability, requiring at least 2^b expected black-box queries to elicit any protected l-gram, together with a worst-case extraction game, a rank-based upper-bound estimator on per-trial extraction probability p_z (Lemma 1: success over n trials <= 1-(1-p*_z)^n; Lemma 2: equivalence to min-entropy H_infinity(D_pro) >= b), and an efficient algorithm that estimates this bound using only the top-m token probabilities and decoding parameters exposed by real LLM APIs (Table 2: OpenAI, Anthropic, Azure, Gemini access patterns); (3) extends the bound from targeted/exact extraction to untargeted and approximate-match extraction; and (4) empirically demonstrates the separation and evaluates candidate defenses.
threat_model: Black-box LLM-API adversary (Figure 1, Section 2.2): the vendor trains model f_theta on dataset D and exposes only an inference oracle O_{f_theta ∘ phi_k,T}. The adversary controls the prompt (prefix z_p) and, per Table 2, some decoding parameters (temperature T, top-k/top-p) and receives at most the top-m token probabilities (m ranges from 0, text-only, up to |V|, full logits; real deployments cap m at 20, e.g. Azure/GPT-4/Gemini). The adversary's goal is to make the oracle emit a protected l-gram subsequence z_s from a protected set D_pro subset of D (Definition 1), either targeted (exact suffix given its true prefix) or untargeted (any protected l-gram), exact or approximate match. The paper explicitly assumes a strong, worst-case adversary who knows the complete target text and can choose the optimal prefix and decoding configuration, arguing this yields a conservative (upper-bound) risk estimate appropriate for auditing rather than a claim about typical attacker capability.
limitations: The paper's own scope notes: (1) the worst-case adversary model assumes full knowledge of the target text and optimal prefix/decoding choice, which is intentionally conservative and may overstate risk relative to realistic attackers with partial knowledge; (2) computing tight min-entropy bounds H_infinity(D_pro) requires strong assumptions about the prior distribution of protected data (e.g., Zipf's law or a conditioned proxy model), and the paper explicitly states these priors 'rely on strong assumptions' and serve only as a reference rather than a precise bound; (3) DP-training defense results show inconsistent reduction across privacy budgets, particularly for short sequences (l=10), which the authors flag as an open question needing further work on the connection between training noise and effective extraction defense; (4) evaluation is limited to GPT-2-Small/Enron and Llama-3.1-8B/Pile+BookSum -- no evaluation against genuinely closed commercial APIs (OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini) despite Table 2 characterizing their access patterns, since real-world experiments used open-source models for 'fully controlled m' instead.

datasets_used: Enron email dataset (fine-tuning target for GPT-2-Small)
The Pile / Pile-CC (pre-training extraction target and non-member split for Llama-3.1-8B / GPT-2)
quantitative_results: (n,p)-discoverable baseline extraction ratio saturates at ~0.015 even at n=10^5 queries due to top-k truncation of ground-truth token rank
Tulu-3 + ParaPO reduces extractable portion at b=32 from 20% (base Llama-3.1-8B) to 0.4%
Tulu-3 + ParaPO combined with API access control (limiting m) further reduces extractable portion at b=32 to 0.03%
Over 99.29% of protected subsequences fall below the in-context 'repeat after me' baseline cost b_ctx
Across 483,485 Pile-CC subsequences (l=50), the Llama-3.1-8B target model's extraction cost exceeds the GPT-2 proxy model's in 398,385 cases (82.4%)
Extraction comparison uses 9x10^5 sliding windows of length l=50 on Llama-3.1-8B/Pile-CC
DP training on GPT-2/Enron reduces extraction ratio but does not scale consistently across privacy budgets epsilon in {1.0, 3.0, 8.0, 20.0}, especially for l=10
baselines_compared: Empirical greedy generation extraction (chunked, fixed short prefix)
Hayes et al. (n,p)-discoverable probabilistic extraction measurement (prior work [8])
Blind extraction-cost baselines: Uniform token sampling, Unigram (empirical frequency), Contextual proxy LM
MIA signals: Loss, Min-K, Zlib, Reference-Loss (used for distinguishability-vs-extractability correlation analysis, not as extraction baselines)
Counterfactual memorization (calibrated memorization score from prior work [37, 50])
